questions.question: In a right triangle, the side opposite angle θ is 5 and the hypotenuse is 13. Find sin(θ).
questions.explanation: SOH-CAH-TOA: sine = opposite/hypotenuse. The opposite side is 5 and the hypotenuse is 13, so sin(θ) = 5/13.

questions.question: A right triangle has an angle of 30° and a hypotenuse of 10. What is the length of the side opposite the 30° angle?
questions.explanation: sin(30°) = 1/2. Using sin(θ) = opposite/hypotenuse: 1/2 = opposite/10, so opposite = 5.

questions.question: Find cos(60°).
questions.explanation: cos(60°) is a standard unit circle value. The adjacent side to a 60° angle in a 30-60-90 triangle is half the hypotenuse.

questions.question: In a right triangle, the adjacent side to angle θ is 7 and the hypotenuse is 14. Find tan(θ).
questions.explanation: First find the opposite side: opposite² + 7² = 14², so opposite = 7√3. Then tan(θ) = opposite/adjacent = 7√3/7 = √3. Actually, cos(θ) = 7/14 = 1/2, so θ = 60°, and tan(60°) = √3.

questions.question: On the unit circle, what is the y-coordinate of the point at angle 90°?
questions.explanation: At 90°, the point on the unit circle is (0, 1). The y-coordinate is sin(90°) = 1.

questions.question: Simplify: sin²(θ) + cos²(θ)
questions.explanation: This is the Pythagorean identity. For any angle θ, sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1.
